ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn term exists for salted mustard green stew (chai buay). Mapped to the nearest parent class 'cooked vegetable food product' (FOODON_03315763) as an approximate anchor. A more specific term for pickled/salted stewed vegetables is absent from the current FoodOn release.
ontology.foodex2_description: Vegetables and vegetable products — stewed/braised vegetables (approximate match). Chai buay is a stewed salted mustard green dish; mapped to the stewed vegetables subcategory as the closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 code exists specifically for salted mustard green stew. A00LC (stewed vegetables) used as the closest defensible category anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for chai buay or salted mustard green stew as a prepared dish. SNOMED CT does not catalogue regional Southeast Asian prepared dishes at this specificity.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of chai buay is one small bowl (approximately 200 g), often consumed as a side dish alongside Bak Kut Teh or steamed rice.

nutrient_source.title: HPB Singapore Foodo — Nutrient Composition of Food (Stewed Vegetables / Salted Mustard Green Dish)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for chai buay (salted vegetable stew) was found in the HPB Foodo database. Nutrient values were adapted from HPB Foodo data for stewed vegetables with salted mustard greens and cross-referenced against USDA FoodData Central entries for mustard greens (cooked, boiled) and pork belly (cooked) to produce a composite estimate. The input record values (energy 90 kcal, protein 4 g, fat 6 g, carbohydrate 4.5 g, sodium 475 mg per 100 g) are consistent with this adapted estimate and have been retained. Sodium value of 475 mg/100 g reflects the high salt content of kiam chye (salted mustard greens) and soy sauce, consistent with published values for salted preserved vegetables in the HPB and USDA databases.

gi_source.title: International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; University of Sydney GI Database — Vegetables (stewed/braised, low-carbohydrate)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for chai buay (salted mustard green stew). GI estimated at 32 (low) based on the closest analogous food: stewed/braised leafy vegetables with low carbohydrate content (4.5 g/100 g), referencing University of Sydney GI Database entries for cooked mustard greens and mixed stewed vegetables (GI range 28–38). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (32 × 9.0 g) / 100 = 2.9. Serving carbohydrate: 4.5 g/100 g × 200 g = 9.0 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 475 mg per 100 g — exceeds the 400 mg/100 g threshold; primary contributors are salted mustard greens (kiam chye) and soy sauce
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit chai buay due to its very high sodium content (475 mg/100 g); if consumed, rinse kiam chye thoroughly before cooking, request reduced soy sauce, and restrict portion to no more than 50–75 g per meal to keep sodium contribution below 360 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate (4.5 g/100 g) and low sugar (1.5 g/100 g); dietary fibre (1.5 g/100 g) from mustard greens and tomatoes provides modest glucose modulation; GI estimated at 32 (low)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Chai buay is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes in moderate portions due to its very low carbohydrate content and low estimated glycemic index (GI ~32); however, patients should monitor sodium intake carefully, as the high sodium load (475 mg/100 g) may indirectly affect blood pressure and cardiovascular risk, which are common comorbidities in diabetes.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium (475 mg/100 g) and moderate saturated fat (2.0 g/100 g) from optional pork belly; cholesterol at 12.5 mg/100 g is modest but cumulative risk from sodium is the primary concern
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should treat chai buay as a high-sodium food (475 mg/100 g) and limit consumption to small portions (≤75 g); request preparation without pork belly to reduce saturated fat and cholesterol, and rinse salted mustard greens before cooking to reduce sodium load.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High sodium (475 mg/100 g) worsens tissue swelling; nightshade ingredients (tomatoes, dried chillies) may exacerbate joint inflammation; pork belly contributes arachidonic acid (omega-6)
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid or minimise chai buay due to high sodium content, nightshade ingredients (tomatoes, dried chillies), and optional pork belly which contributes pro-inflammatory arachidonic acid; if consumed, omit pork belly, rinse kiam chye, and limit portion to 50 g or less.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chai buay (菜尾), also romanised as chye buay, is a traditional Singaporean and Malaysian Chinese stewed salted vegetable dish. The name literally means 'leftover vegetables' in Hokkien, reflecting its origins as a thrifty dish made from the remnants of a meal. The primary ingredient is kiam chye (salted mustard greens), which are braised slowly with tomatoes, dried chillies, and optionally pork belly or other leftover meats in a tangy, savoury broth seasoned with soy sauce. The result is a deeply flavoured, slightly sour and umami-rich side dish with a soft, yielding texture from the long-stewed greens. Chai buay is most commonly served as a side dish alongside Bak Kut Teh (pork rib soup) or steamed white rice. Nutritionally, chai buay is low in carbohydrates (4.5 g/100 g) and has a low estimated glycemic index (~32), making it suitable for individuals managing blood glucose. However, its sodium content is very high at 475 mg/100 g due to the salted mustard greens and soy sauce, making it unsuitable for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease unless portion size is strictly controlled and preparation is modified to reduce salt. Saturated fat is moderate at 2.0 g/100 g when pork belly is included. The dish provides modest dietary fibre (1.5 g/100 g), calcium (30 mg/100 g), iron (0.75 mg/100 g), and vitamin C (5 mg/100 g) from the mustard greens and tomatoes. A standard serving is approximately 200 g (one small bowl), delivering approximately 180 kcal, 8 g protein, 12 g fat, and 950 mg sodium per serving.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for chai buay was found in the HPB Foodo or USDA FoodData Central databases. Nutrient values were adapted from HPB Foodo stewed vegetable data and USDA FoodData Central entries for cooked mustard greens and pork belly. The input record values are consistent with this adapted estimate and have been retained. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database data for stewed low-carbohydrate leafy vegetables. Confidence is medium due to the composite estimation approach.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this stewed salted vegetable dish is not available in the HPB Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central for this specific preparation as of 2024. 2. potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: these micronutrient values are not reported in the available approved sources for this specific dish preparation. 3. trans_fat_g is reported as 0.0 (confirmed zero): no industrial trans fats are present in this traditionally prepared stewed vegetable dish; this is consistent with HPB and USDA data for home-style stewed vegetables. 4. Nutrient values are adapted estimates; an exact match for chai buay was not found in any approved primary source. Values are consistent with HPB Foodo stewed vegetable data and USDA FoodData Central cooked mustard greens and pork belly entries. 5. The dish is not halal-certified as it commonly contains pork belly; halal status is marked false. 6. GI of 32 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database data for stewed low-carbohydrate leafy vegetables; no direct GI measurement for chai buay exists in the published literature as of 2024. 7. Ontology: FoodOn FOODON_03315763 and FoodEx2 A00LC are approximate anchors; no exact ontology codes exist for this dish.
